data.title: Understanding CLOB: Income From The Middle Of The CLO Structure
data.description:
The VanEck AA-BB CLO ETF (CLOB) is an actively managed ETF launched in September 2024. It buys AA to BB rated CLO debt tranches. Its goal is current income with capital preservation. CLOB charges 0.45% and pays monthly distributions, currently 6.59%. CLOB's AUM is $152.36 million, with 52 holdings as of January 10, 2026.

This mezzanine-level CLO debt can pay higher yields compared to similarly rated corporate bonds. That is because they come with certain additional risks not present in corporate bonds. However, CLOB comes with its own set of protections as well, which include overcollateralization (OC) tests and interest-coverage (IC) tests. These are designed to limit credit losses before they hit higher tranches.

What is a CLO?

“CLO” stands for “Collateralized Loan Obligation.” A CLO owns senior secured loans as collateral and issues multiple layers (“tranches”) of securities against that collateral. It then pays cash flows on a priority basis (“the waterfall”). CLOB does not buy the underlying loans directly; instead, it buys CLO tranches—the debt slices issued by CLO vehicles—across the AA and BB stack.

This has two practical consequences. First, this positions you one step removed from the borrower. The CLO structure—its protections, manager actions, and deal tests—sits in between. So your credit experience is coming from this CLO structure and not just the loan default rate.

Second, ratings distributions become important. VanEck’s portfolio has the following: AA 42.02%, A 22.85%, BBB 20.98%, and BB 13.49%, with 0.69% not rated (and a total investment grade share of 85.86%). This tells you where you sit in the credit stack and how strongly you are protected before losses reach you. The more weight given to higher-rated tranches, the less likely that loan losses will hurt cash flow or principal—at the fund level and at your investor level. CLOB lives mainly in the “upper mezzanine” and “lower IG” parts of the CLO debt stack, although it still goes into BB when the spread becomes interesting.

Sources of Distributions

CLOB’s distribution history helps us understand how the amounts and sources of distribution can vary month to month. It also tells us how CLO tranche cash flows and trading dynamics actually work.

Here are a few select examples of 2025 monthly distributions: $0.3049 on July 1, $0.3046 on August 1, $0.2754 on September 2, $0.2640 on October 1, $0.2836 on November 3, $0.2629 on November 28, and $0.3271 on December 29. November shows two distributions due to calendar timing, a common feature of monthly ETFs, rather than a change in underlying income. Annual payout (TTM) was $3.35 per share, or roughly 6.6% TTM yield.

As you can see, there’s a bit of variability month-to-month in the distributions. This variability comes from how these distributions are sourced. These are mainly two: net interest income from CLO tranches and realized gains or losses from trading those tranches.

VanEck’s distribution history helps understand this better since it also describes the composition breakdown of the distribution. For example, in the December 29, 2025 distribution, VanEck reports that $0.2630 of the payment was classified as dividend income, while $0.0641 was classified as short-term capital gains, with no return of capital.

This is an important distinction. CLOB’s core way to generate returns is the floating-rate coupon income coming from the CLO debt. But the manager (PineBridge) can also produce gains (or losses) by buying and selling the portfolio as spreads tighten (or widen). These CLO tranches are traded instruments, and their prices move with credit spreads, rate expectations, and relative value within the capital stack. When there are gains made, investors get a share as part of the monthly distribution. This is why distributions vary month to month.

Portfolio Holdings

From the fund fact sheet, here’s a quick list of some of the top holdings, along with coupons, maturities, and weights (along with a T-bill position):

  • Octagon 64 Ltd 5.570% maturity 07/21/2037 at 6.66% of net assets

  • Rockford Tower CLO 2022-1 Ltd 5.584% maturity 07/20/2035 at 4.67%

  • Dryden 95 CLO Ltd 6.939% maturity 08/20/2034 at 4.61%

  • United States Treasury Bill maturity 01/27/2026 at 4.59%

  • Several other CLO positions around 3.33%–3.35% each

The top ten holdings account for 41.55% of the portfolio.

From this list, we see that many of the top holdings have long legal maturities. However, these are floating rate coupons, so cash flow keeps changing regularly as rates change. That long maturity date simply tells you how long the deal lasts. We also see a bit of cash/T-bill, which is useful to avoid selling less liquid CLO tranches at lower prices when shares are created or redeemed.

Why these tranches pay more than corporate bonds

CLOB’s core goal is that it will yield more than similarly rated corporate bonds while still retaining built-in protection against credit loss. That extra yield is basically what investors earn for the additional risks—which don’t show up clearly if you only compare AA-rated CLOs to AA-rated corporate bonds. There are some practical reasons CLO debt can pay more than similarly rated corporates:

Complexity premium: Corporate bonds are easier to understand—one issuer, one balance sheet. CLO tranches are complex—a pooled loan basket, active management, reinvestment rules, structural tests, the works. That means fewer buyers will want to invest, so spreads widen.

Liquidity premium: In comparison to corporate bonds, CLO tranches may trade in instruments with much lower liquidity, even though ratings may be similar. Investors demand extra spread as compensation for that liquidity risk.

Reputational risk: CLOs are quite different from pre-crisis subprime CDOs, but they are both “structured finance.” So they may seem risky by association and keep investors away. This could widen spreads even when “default probability” may not warrant it. That means it could widen the yield even when ratings are the same.

Structural risk is different from issuer risk: CLOs are exposed to other sorts of risks like collateral quality migration, manager behavior, and deal test dynamics. It is not just the simple probability of one issuer defaulting. So even if both have the same rating, risk difference = premium difference.

This is important to understand: the extra yield you get comes at a price—extra risk and difficult-to-model risk. Many investors want to avoid that by default.

Those “built-in protections” and what they really mean

VanEck assures us that CLOB comes with “built-in protection against credit loss.” Don’t treat that phrase as a guarantee; it simply means that the fund’s CLO tranches are positioned above layers of subordinate capital. They are also governed by structural safeguards such as overcollateralization and interest-coverage tests that are designed to absorb losses before they reach senior investors.

What you can safely say (based on what’s shown):

  • CLOB mostly owns investment-grade tranches (~85.86% at last read);
  • Because these are tranches, losses will first hit the most subordinate layers before they hit higher layers—this is the core “cushion” logic;
  • The fund very clearly buys from AA+ down to BB- (or unrated but comparable quality).

What you cannot say is that CLOB is “safe like Treasuries.” The fund holds (97.15%) securitized credit, meaning its returns are exposed to credit spreads, liquidity issues, and structured-finance dynamics. Even when underlying loan performance remains stable, CLO tranche prices can fluctuate as risk appetite and market conditions change.

Peer Positioning: CLOB Versus AAA CLO ETFs

Seeking Alpha’s peer comparison places CLOB alongside CLO ETFs that invest almost exclusively in AAA-rated CLO tranches. The three names I want to highlight are Invesco AAA CLO Floating Rate Note ETF (ICLO), iShares AAA CLO Active ETF (CLOA), and PGIM AAA CLO ETF (PAAA).

These AAA CLO ETFs sit at the top of the capital structure. They are the last to absorb credit losses. They emphasize capital preservation, with yield being the trade-off. Recent yield data for these three are 5.48%, 5.34%, and 5.11%, respectively. These sit at the most protected level of the stack, so the spread is the narrowest.

CLO deliberately stays below AAA. As I discussed earlier, this positioning adds new risks while still retaining meaningful protection. In exchange, CLOB offers higher income, at around 6.5%, roughly 100-150 basis points higher than AAA-only CLO ETFs.

That 1-1.5% yield gap may look modest, but it is not. Relative to a 5.3% baseline, a 100 bps extra means nearly 20% extra income, which compounds meaningfully over time. More importantly, there’s not really a dramatic increase in expected default loss between AAA and AA-BB. It is simply meaningful extra compensation for moving down the capital stack without a meaningful increase in risks in most situations. Briefly then, AAA CLO ETFs primarily focus on capital preservation and liquidity, while CLOB is an income-oriented structured-credit strategy.

It is also useful to contrast CLOB with CLO equity-focused funds like Eagle Point Credit Company (ECC), Oxford Lane Capital (OXLC), and OFS Credit Company (OCCI). These invest in the equity tranche that lies at the bottom of the capital stack in terms of loss risks. CLO equity absorbs first losses and gets only whatever cash flow remains but can receive very high yields. OXLC and ECC, for example, have current yields of 32.52% and 29.96%, respectively. CLOB, on the other hand, has a much lower yield but sits higher in the structure and gets paid before equity. Each has its value at different market conditions, with CLOB serving as a balance of sorts.

Key Risks to Understand

CLOB comes with mostly IG-grade ratings and built-in protections, but it is still a structured credit instrument and carries risks that are different from those of traditional bonds.

Spread and liquidity risk: CLOB is priced mainly on credit spreads, not interest rates. When the market is risk averse, spreads can widen. Now, since these tranches have relatively lower liquidity, prices can move suddenly. This can happen even when there are no realized credit losses.

Reinvestment and vintage risk: How a CLO performs is partly dependent on how loan cash flows are reinvested. Future cash flows get affected by loan spreads, underwriting standards, and market conditions. Therefore, CLOs with the same ratings can perform differently if issued in different market conditions.

Manager and structural risk: CLOB is actively managed. Managers select the loans; they make trading decisions, and so on. So, CLOB’s performance may be influenced by its managers. On top of that, structural features such as OC/IC tests vary by deal. They can go down if collateral quality falls.

Conclusion

CLOB is best seen as an income-focused instrument concentrated in the upper mezzanine of the CLO capital stack. They are not substitutes for traditional bonds or cash. CLOB primarily invests in AA to BBB tranches, with some BB when spreads are good. They get a 1-1.5% yield premium from structure, subordination, and market frictions over AAA-rated ETFs, not because of dramatically higher expected credit losses.

On the other hand, that yield advantage is not trivial, as I have outlined here. They come with meaningful protection. Returns come mostly from carry, with periodic pass-through realized gains also contributing.

In terms of safety, therefore, CLOB sits near but below T-bills but much above equity CLOs. For investors who understand CLO waterfalls and are comfortable with some volatility in exchange for higher income, CLOB occupies a clear and defensible place in an income-oriented portfolio.

data.title: All Is Not Well On The Japanese Bond Market Front
data.description:
It should come as no surprise that the Japanese government bond market appears to be melting down and that the Japanese yen is very much on the back foot. After all, Sanae Takaichi, the new Japanese prime minister, is proposing a reckless budget policy and early elections at a time when her country is drowning in debt. What is surprising, however, is how calm world financial markets seem to be about the troubles surfacing in the world’s second-largest government bond market with a total outstanding debt of over $7 trillion. A full-blown Japanese government bond and currency market crisis could have major spillover effects on the rest of the world’s capital market and could renew US-Japanese trade tensions.

Anyone doubting that a Japanese government bond and currency market crisis is brewing has not been paying attention. Since March 2024, when the Bank of Japan’s (BOJ) yield curve control policy ended, the 10-year Japanese government bond yield has approximately tripled from 0.75 percent to 2.18 percent. That rate is the highest in the past 20 years. Meanwhile, over the past nine months, the Japanese yen has weakened by more than 10 percent from 142 yen to the dollar in April 2025 to around 159 yen to the dollar at present. That rate is the weakest for the Japanese yen in more than three decades and is bound to raise red flags in Washington about an unduly weak Japanese yen.

The underlying reason for the recent Japanese government bond market weakness is the abandonment of the BOJ’s yield control policy due to rising domestic inflation and the very poor state of the country’s public finances. At around 230 percent of GDP, Japan’s public debt is by far the highest among the industrialized countries. There appears to be little prospect that this debt mountain will be reduced anytime soon. The government continues to run a primary budget deficit, and the country’s long-term economic growth prospects are highly constrained by its poor demographics. With a shrinking workforce, Japan’s long-run economic growth potential is generally estimated to be below one percent.

Seeming to be casting caution to the wind, Sanae Takaichi is proposing a US$135 billion budget stimulus package that would add to the country’s budget deficit. She also appears to be on the verge of calling a snap election to strengthen her hold on power. Those elections would delay the budget’s approval for this fiscal year and would add pressure to the government bond and currency markets.

All of this puts the BOJ in an unenviable position. With inflation running at three percent, or above the BOJ’s two percent inflation target, and with the government pursuing an expansionary budget policy, the BOJ can ill-afford to reduce interest rates. At the same time, any effort to stabilize the currency and to fight inflation with higher interest rates would add to the budget deficit by increasing the government’s interest payment expenditures. That would raise further questions about Japanese public debt sustainability.

Against this background, it is difficult to understand how sanguine world financial markets are about Japan’s bond market troubles. Should those troubles deepen, one would think that they would induce global investors to scale back on risk and to cast their eye on other countries, not least the United States that also has unsustainable public finances. One would also think that markets would become concerned about the unwinding of the Japanese yen currency trade, whereby investors borrowed in yen to invest in higher-yielding international bonds. A repatriation of those funds would deprive the world of a major pillar of support that their financial markets have enjoyed from Japanese investment abroad. That would especially be the case if the Japanese banks were forced to sell their US government bond holdings to help cope with the large unrealized losses that they have on their Japanese government bond holdings.

Markets also seem to be ignoring the risk that a weakening Japanese yen could invite a US protectionist backlash against Japan. Heightening the chances of such a backlash is the fact that Japan is running a large bilateral trade surplus with the United States.

In short, troubles in the Japanese bond and currency market could have spillover effects on the rest of the world economy. World financial markets seem to be ignoring those risks at their peril.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Extreme Networks: Ruckus Acquisition Would Be Massive, If It's True
data.description:
Introduction

It’s been quite a while since I last covered Extreme Networks, Inc. (EXTR), so I thought this would be a perfect time to look at the company’s performance over the last while, now that there has been some news regarding a potential acquisition.

If the deal does not come to life, I think I would like to continue to monitor the company’s organic growth and profitability before I would change my rating.

Last time I covered it was back in September of 2024, and since then, the company’s shares appreciated by only around 8% compared to 21% of the broader index SPY (SPY). I am maintaining a Hold rating.

Briefly On Performance

Let’s begin from the top. Over the past two or so years, the company’s sales have been on quite a rollercoaster. In 2024, we can see that sales began to decline, which stemmed from the post-COVID channel inventory digestion and overall weak demand. Distributors reduced purchases so they could clear the excess inventory buildup, and the post-pandemic slowdown in IT spending caused the company to experience longer sales cycles and some pushout. Many customers decided to defer buying amid economic uncertainty. So, 2024 was not EXTR’s year. Looking at the 2025 performance, we can see that all the inventory problems are more or less resolved after 2024, which enabled normalized ordering and sequential growth over the next quarters, with the help of AI and Regional demand. EXTR’s Platform One saw strong early traction, and bookings for Q4 ’25 are at two-year highs due to key government and education wins.

With a return in sales growth, we can see the company’s margins followed suit. The company became GAAP profitable once again, although just barely. In the latest quarter, we can see that the company’s GAAP operating margin improved to 3.6%, while its adjusted operating margin improved 110bps to 13.3%. Gross margins took a slight hit this past quarter due to cost pressures that saw sharp price hikes due to supply constraints and tariffs. But other than that, the company has been performing better this past year. Let’s hope they can keep this momentum going.

Regarding the company’s financial position, EXTR finished Q1 ‘26 with around $209m in cash and equivalents, against $159m in long-term debt. The cash on hand can easily cover the company’s debt, and since the company is returning to profitability, its interest coverage ratio is already at over 3x, which is more than sustainable. I expect this ratio to improve further, given how it looks like its profitability is just beginning its run.

So overall, the company seems to be at the beginning of a healthy turnaround over the past year. The company’s slightly better mix regarding its offerings that leaned more towards the SaaS side of the business helped push it into profitability once again, and seeing how the SaaS segment has performed recently, I believe we will see further improvements going forward.

Comments On The Outlook

For the next quarter, EXTR expects to see sales in the range of $309m-$315m, gross margins in the range of 61.4%-62%, and adjusted operating margins in the range of 13.4%-14.6%, with adjusted EPS in the range of $0.23-$0.25. A more detailed look can be seen below, where on a GAAP basis, it looks more of the same.

The management expects SaaS ARR growth to be in the low 20% range for the year, and recurring revenue to be around 35% of total revenues in 2026. In general, total revenue growth is expected to accelerate to 10% due to “growing volume of large opportunities” and “increasing winning percentage,” calling 2026 “an inflection point” in terms of its growth trajectory. So, recurring revenue in terms of total revenues remains more or less the same.

What is going to push the company’s operations to something more exciting?

Ruckus Acquisition: Still Very Early, But Could Be Massive

The most recent development is the rumored attempt to acquire Ruckus from CommScope (COMM), or, as it is called now, Vistance Networks (VISN). EXTR is exploring a cash-and-stock deal, which could put Ruckus at around $1B valuation.

This acquisition would bolster EXTR’s enterprise Wi-Fi and wired networking portfolio, which will target high-density environments like stadiums, hotels, schools, and government. Ruckus seems to have a massive advantage there. This is not a small deal at all. At an over $1B proposed valuation, it is around 50% of EXTR’s current $2.1B market cap, so it is quite an ambitious move.

The synergies here include product expansion, as Ruckus will add its specialized Wi-Fi access points, switches, and software to EXTR’s wired/wireless/SW-WAN lineup, which creates a more robust coverage throughout, converging platforms.

As both focus on serving large enterprises, the federal government, and financial services, there will be a potential for cross-selling products, like cloud-managed services and Platform ONE integration. Additionally, Ruckus revenue model is in line with EXTR’s subscription/SaaS ambitions, which could potentially accelerate SaaS ARR growth in the future.

All of this sounds very good; however, it is all still a rumor. Neither company came out officially and confirmed nor denied this speculation, so take it all with a grain of salt. It would be a pretty massive deal for EXTR as it is not a chump change acquisition. The company will have to dilute its shareholders and take on additional debt, which will put some pressure on the company’s overall operational performance, with the hopes that integration goes through with minimal hiccups.

Will we see some regulatory pushback if the speculations are true? I don’t expect it to be a massive hurdle. But since the deal size is going to be around $1B, an HSR filing will be necessary, which means FTC/DOJ review will be required, but since there is very minimal overlap in both of the businesses, scrutiny will be reduced.

Investor Takeaway

I’m going to see how the next few quarters pan out for EXTR before considering changing my rating, which may not be particularly necessary if the deal does not come true.

I also want to see what kind of operational efficiencies the company achieves over the next few quarters, and whether the profitability it currently has can be sustained or improved upon.

data.title: PSCT: Technology Sector Dashboard For January
data.description:
This article offers a top-down view of the information technology sector based on value, quality, and momentum metrics. It may also help to analyze sector ETFs such as The Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLK) and iShares U.S. Technology ETF (IYW), whose holdings are used to calculate these metrics. This article will focus on a lesser-known ETF focusing on smaller tech companies: Invesco S&P SmallCap Information Technology ETF (PSCT).

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italics describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield (“EY”), Sales Yield (“SY”), Free Cash Flow Yield (“FY”), Return on Equity (“ROE”), and Gross Margin (“GM”). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on the trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY, and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales, and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non-available when the “something” is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set into a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value And Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively as EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for hardware in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in hardware companies.

The Value Score (“VS”) is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score (“QS”) is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.

Current Data

The next table shows the metrics and scores as of writing. Columns stand for all the data defined above.

Value And Quality chart

The next chart plots the Value and Quality Scores by industry (higher is better).

Evolution Since Last Month

The most notable change is a deterioration of value score for the computer & hardware industry.

Momentum

The next chart plots momentum scores based on median returns.

Interpretation

Based on my latest S&P 500 dashboard, the information technology sector is overvalued by 25% relative to 11-year averages. All subsectors are not equal, though. The software industry is close to its historical baseline in valuation and quality, and IT services are moderately overvalued, while hardware and semiconductors are deeply overpriced.

Fast Facts On PSCT

Invesco S&P SmallCap Information Technology ETF was launched on 04/07/2010 and tracks the S&P SmallCap 600 Capped Information Technology Index. PSCT has a portfolio of 70 stocks and an expense ratio of 0.29%, while the large-cap sector benchmark XLK charges only 0.08%. The portfolio is composed of constituents of the S&P SmallCap 600 Index classified in the GICS information technology sector.

The top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 33.8% of asset value, and the largest position weighs 4.5%. The fund is better diversified and has lower company risk than XLK, where the top 10 names weigh 61.2% and the three largest positions are between 10% and 15% (Nvidia, Apple, and Microsoft).

In summary, PSCT offers lower company risk and better value than capital-weighted large-cap funds in technology. However, historical returns point to massive underperformance of small caps in the sector. PSCT may be part of tactical allocation strategies, but it doesn’t look very attractive as a long-term investment.

Dashboard List

I use the first table to calculate value and quality scores. It may also be used in a stock-picking process to check how companies stand among their peers. For example, the EY column tells us that an IT services company with an earnings yield above 0.0291 (or price/earnings below 34.36) is in the better half of the industry regarding this metric. A Dashboard List is sent every month to Quantitative Risk & Value subscribers, with the most profitable companies standing in the better half among their peers regarding the three valuation metrics at the same time. The stocks below are part of the list sent to subscribers a few weeks ago.

UPWK

TDC

YOU

Clear Secure, Inc.

It is a rotational model with a statistical bias toward excess returns in the long term, not the result of in-depth stock analysis.

data.title: Inside The Annual Style Indices Rebalance: Methodology, Process And Outcomes
data.description:
By Cristopher Anguiano

The S&P U.S. Style Indices completed their annual rebalance in December 2025. Exhibit 1 shows that the 2025 changes were less pronounced than prior years: turnover for the S&P 500® Growth and S&P 500 Value was lower than the past three annual rebalances. Nonetheless, there were several shifts in index composition.

The most significant movement was in Information Technology. Post-rebalance, Information Technology companies accounted for nearly half of the S&P 500 Growth, an increase of 7.5% (see Exhibit 2). This gain was driven by the reclassification of some of the largest S&P 500 companies, supported by strong earnings, sales growth and momentum factors. Meanwhile, the weights of Consumer Discretionary, Industrials and Consumer Staples collectively declined by 6.8%.

Although the post-rebalance Information Technology and Communication Services weights in the S&P 500 Growth rank among the highest since the current S&P U.S. Style Indices’ approach to defining style was introduced in 2009, these levels are not unprecedented. For instance, Information Technology companies accounted for 55.3% of the index in August 2000 (see Exhibit 3). Likewise, Energy’s absence from the S&P 500 Growth after the 2025 reconstitution is not unprecedented - this last occurred in May 2002, following a long-term decline in its weight. Specifically, energy companies exhibited stronger price-to-book, price-to-earnings and sales-to-price ratios alongside weaker sales and earnings growth. These shifts underscore how the S&P U.S. Style Indices can reflect prevailing market trends, with AI currently serving as a key driver of the U.S. Information Technology and Communication Services sectors, while mostly lower, more volatile commodity prices shaped the reclassification of energy companies after record profitability in 2021-2022.

While Financials has traditionally dominated the S&P 500 Value, Information Technology’s growing influence in the U.S. large-cap segment has reshaped the index composition. Since the 2024 rebalance, Information Technology has held the largest sector weight in the S&P 500 Value and maintained that leadership throughout 2025 (see Exhibit 4).

Although the reclassification of various tech companies contributed to Information Technology’s weight falling by 8.5% at the latest rebalance, the sector continued to be the largest in the S&P 500 Value (see Exhibit 5). Financials was the second-largest sector, and its weight increased at the latest rebalance. Consumer Discretionary’s weight rose by 3.1%, reaching its highest post-rebalance weight in the S&P 500 Value since 2009 and ranking as the third-largest sector at 11.3%. Industrials (up 2.7%) and Consumer Staples (up 2.2%) also saw their weight increase.

These shifts highlight the growing influence of Information Technology within the S&P 500 and illustrate how sector weights adjust to changes in company fundamentals and market dynamics. While these trends explain much of the rebalance impact, the underlying methodology remains the foundation of the index composition. Understanding these mechanics can offer valuable insight into how growth and value indices evolve over time and may also help with interpreting changes beyond headline numbers.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit www.spdji.com. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Tutor Perini: Backlog Is Finally Turning Into Cash
data.description:
Investment Thesis

My thesis on Tutor Perini Corporation (TPC) is simple. The company is moving from a period where legacy disputes and uneven profitability dominated the narrative into a period where newer, larger and higher margin projects dominate the income statement and more importantly, the cashflow statement. Q3 2025 operating cashflows came in at $289 Mn and operating cashflow for the first time in nine months of 2025 hit $574Mn. These have both been described as records by the management. I am also rating TPC a Strong buy because I think the market is still treating it like a messy contractor story. And yet, the numbers are starting to look like a cash conversion story. And that difference matters. A lot. In Q3 2025, management did not just talk about improvement. And in addition to the record numbers, they also raised guidance again and pointed to a multi-year visibility that is sitting in plain sight inside a record backlog. I believe that is the sort of combo that tends to rerate slowly at first, then quickly once investors stop anchoring an old headache. I also believe that this kind of cash generation changes the whole conversation because it funds de-risking. It funds debt reduction, and it funds optionality like share repurchases and dividends.

Now, I'll say the quiet part out loud. Construction names don't get excitement until cash shows up because a backlog without cash is like a promise without confirmation. What caught my eye here with TPC is that cash is showing up in size, while debt is coming down and the backlog is still growing. As of now, management is even talking about dividends and buybacks as a near term 'when' and not a distant 'maybe' which is a strong signal on confidence, I would say. And as a result, this kind of set up has plenty of ways to work. It can work through earnings growth. It can also work through cashflows, and it can work through multiple expansion once the market stops pricing the past.

The second leg of my thesis is visibility and mix. Tutor Perini's backlog climbed to a new record of $21.6Bn, a 54% increase y/y and that backlog is heavily skewed toward civil. It is important to mention that this is the highest margin segment. Civil backlog alone was $10.5Bn which is 49% of total backlog and the company has been explicit that civil segment margins have historically been 8% to 12%. Yet, they are expected to run 12% to 15% in 2026. I like stories where the highest margin engine is also the biggest part of the order book. That's where operating leverage becomes less of a theory and more of a math problem.

The last leg is the surprise factor. Seeking Alpha's growth metrics paint a picture that most investors don't associate with construction contractors. The company shows 19.22% y/y revenue growth and 17.01% FWD revenue growth as shown in the figure below. And the operating cashflows lines are even louder, with operating cashflows at 199.69% and levered FCFs growth at 148%. Those numbers are definitely not 'steady utility contractor' numbers. They are the kind of numbers you usually see when a business shifts from being working capital hungry to being working capital productive.

Business Overview

Tutor Perini is a diversified construction firm that does general contracting, design build and a meaningful amount of self-perform work. The company is headquartered in Los Angeles and has a long operating history plus a national footprint and some targeted international exposure through areas like the Indo-Pacific. It reports through three key segments; Civil, Building and Specialty Contractors. And the mix matters because civil is positioned as the profitability driver. As of Q3, the company had a revenue of $5.1Bn on a twelve-month basis and record $21.6Bn backlog that gives multi-year revenue visibility. That backlog is split across $10.5Bn Civil, $7.9Bn Building and $3.2Bn Specialty Contractors, which tells me that this is not a one project wonder. It is a portfolio of megaprojects.

Growth Drivers

The biggest catalyst is simple. Execution on a record backlog that is already tilted toward higher margin work. Backlog reached $21.6 Bn in Q3 2025 and management said they added $2Bn of new award and contract adjustments in the quarter with a 1.4x book to burn for Q3. I also note that TPC had a 1.7x book to burn for the first nine months of 2025 which tells me that this backlog is just stable. It is growing. And with the revenue of $5.1Bn, the backlog is almost 4x the revenue base. I believe that is multi-year visibility, even if awards slowed tomorrow. In a market where investors say they want certainty, backlog is as close as construction gets to certainty.

The other detail I like is certainty. Management highlighted that they have well over $25Bn of upcoming bidding opportunities over the next 12 to 18 months. This includes the $12Bn Sepulveda Transit Corridor in California and the $5bN Penn Station transformation in New York. And this tells me that Tutor Perini is certainly competing, where project size and complexity narrow the field. And that's definitely a nice place to be when infrastructure dollars are flowing.

The second catalyst is cash conversion and balance sheet cleanup. In Q3, operating cashflow was extraordinarily strong at $289Mn. This was also a record number and a fourth consecutive year of record operating cash generation. Now, this matters because it's not only about a good year. It suggests that an operating rhythm has changed. And it means that the company's cash position is healthy. And for sure, that's a different tone than a company still fighting for oxygen. It sounds like a company preparing to choose how it wants to reward shareholders.

Valuation

Interestingly, TPC's valuation grade shows a D+ valuation grade according to SA Factor grades. Nevertheless, when I look under the hood, the story is that it is not expensive. It is what I would call 'messy metrics'. For instance, the company's FWD Non-GAAP P/E is at 17.96x, which is about 18% below the sector median of 22.11x. FWD EV/EBITDA is 11.71x, slightly below the sector median of 12.88x. And FWD EV/EBIT is 11.97x, 35% below the sector median of 18.6x. When I look at those numbers, those are not bargain-basement multiples, but they are also not the multiples of a stock that the market thinks is about to fall apart. They read like a stock that has not fully priced in a cleaner earnings and cash flow profile, in my view.

The value shows up even more clearly when I look at sales and cashflow multiples. The FWD EV/Sales multiple is at 0.69x, compared to sector median of 2.31x. The FWD Price/sales is at 0.72x versus 1.92x sector median. The TTM price/cashflow multiple is 4.29x versus 15.17x for the sector median. This implies a very different cash flow yield profile than what the market is assigning to the average company in the group.

From management guidance, I note that the 2025 adjusted EPS guidance was revived to $4.0 and $4.2. I also note that management said that adjusted EPS in 2026 and 2027 should be significantly higher than the upper end of that 2025 range. Now, if I pencil in even a conservative step up from around $4.1 in 2025 to something like $5.25 to $5.75 in 2026, then I am looking at roughly 28% to 40% growth. And when I pair that with an 18.13x FWD Non-GAAP P/E, I arrive at an implied PEG that can easily land below 1.0x under reasonable assumptions, but that's not a promise. It is a way to see that the valuation is not obviously stretched relative to the growth profile management is guiding towards.

Key Risks

The first risk is that project execution can turn quickly, especially on large fixed price or complex jobs where scope, schedule, labor, and materials shift. Tutor Perini itself highlights risks around revisions of contract estimates, extra work outside scope, schedule requirements, and disputes that can impact working capital, profit, and cashflow. If a major project runs into delays or cost overruns, then there could be margin compressions and negative surprises that hit the share price fast.

Secondly, I would say it is dispute resolution and collection timing. Management was clear that Q3 operating cashflow strength was driven almost entirely by collections from newer and ongoing projects, and they also described the ongoing resolution of disputed matters as a driver of improvements in balance sheet items like CIE. That's usually a good sign when it goes in TPC's way. It can be quite frustrating when it drags. If dispute resolutions slow down or counterparties delay payments, cash conversion can flatten, and the market tends to punish that because it hates uncertainty.

Final Thoughts

At the end of the day, I am rating Tutor Perini a strong buy because I see a company that's finally getting paid, not just getting promised. Record backlog gives visibility. Civil mix gives margin support, and record cash generation gives optionality. The market is still pricing the stock like a low-quality contractor, yet valuation metrics show low sales and cashflow multiples compared to sector medians, even while FWD earnings and EV multiples look reasonable.

data.title: Energy Fuels: Fixing The Rare Earth Bottleneck
data.description:
I last covered Energy Fuels Inc. (UUUU), a uranium and rare earth mineral miner, in October. That was the third piece of coverage I've given to UUUU, and an upgrade.

You can see my full coverage history here, but here's an illustration:

Just as in that first article in November 2024, I feel like there are catalysts everywhere for Energy Fuels, and I don't see this latest run-up as just hype, but because of new catalysts that are bringing the firm closer to profitability and more gains.

Before we get into the study that UUUU just dropped about its rare earth operations, it's important to cover the rare earth story and what's going on with those catalysts. Here's the macro picture.

The Rare Earth Stockpile Bill Is Another Step

Political catalysts for rare earth element miners (and critical minerals miners at large) keep coming, with news dropping of a bipartisan bill going up for a vote (with large existing support, it seems) that proposes building a stockpile of rare earth elements. Washington intends to build it up to the tune of $2.5B.

This is on top of the existing investments already being made into rare earth miners themselves, totaling over $4.5B so far. I covered this government intervention in the rare earths market in a previous article, "The Critical Materials Rally Will Continue."

I suspect that one of the ways in which the US will continue to fight back against [the asymmetry of rare earth access with the Chinese]...is to invest further into the sector, like they already have with MP Materials (MP), Lithium Americas (LAC), and Trilogy Metals (TMQ). The Department of Defense (War?) has been investing in these operations, giving them the necessary liquidity and impetus to expand operations and close the gaps in our materials mining and processing.

It's classic U.S. policy to try throwing money at a problem first and foremost, and for those willing to dig through firms and invest in the winners, this move from the government creates a put of sorts, a protection on the price of the market; the government is an unfair buyer, it has access to the money printer.

Washington, in the case of MP, has agreed to set a price floor on magnet-producing rare earths that is above the going market price (almost double). They are intentionally paying more to make it profitable for MP to operate, because they can't compete with Chinese operations naturally.

This is not necessarily a bad thing from a nationalist's perspective (so perhaps it matters whether you lie on the globalist or nationalist axis of the geopolitical issue with China), even if it subverts the free market. They rationalize it by saying that this market is being distorted to retaliate against China subsidizing their own rare earth miners and to create U.S. independence from China in the sector so they can't weaponize access against us, like they recently did with export controls.

So this stockpile is the next step in the U.S. game plan to decouple from China in the rare earth market, and I doubt it will fail; I give it an 80% chance to pass unabated.

The bill has bipartisan support so far and very likely has the approval of the White House, which has been setting the previous policy of price floors and direct investments in mining companies. Funding would not be difficult to secure among a several-trillion dollar deficit, I'm sure.

I am not trying to take on side on the debate of whether market rigging in the face of potential Chinese belligerence is right or wrong, but any strategic thinker that sees this chart should be alarmed. China and Russia control the world's rare earths processing.

Folks who do not transact in rare earths may be surprised to learn that they are not incredibly expensive per ounce like gold, or even silver. This price is akin to trying to buy up over half the market!

$2.5B will make a massive impact on a market barely hitting $4B in 2024.

The Rare Earth Bottleneck

Now let's drill into the micro picture, on UUUU specifically.

Energy Fuels is already the largest uranium miner in the U.S., and is working toward expanding its rare earth operations, both in mining and processing, the important bottleneck to address. The chart above with the New Axis is only showing processing capacity, not necessarily raw materials. We actually have access to most of the rare earths that we need in raw form, but just lack access to the processing.

There's a good reason for this. Processing is very dirty, and the ability to process rare earths cleanly just hasn't existed until recently. The West eschewed processing because it's dirty; Arizona is beautiful, and parts of Inner Mongolia look like Mordor.

It can't be helped for the most part; processing a ton of graphite produces several tons of contaminated water, and nearly 100 tons of dirty water that could never be considered potable again. This is true for most rare earths (that processing produces a lot of waste), though graphite is the most extreme example.

This is why the release that Energy Fuels made on Jan. 15th was so important: they released a Bankable Feasibility Study ("BFS") on the White Mesa mill in Utah, a plant wholly owned by UUUU.

The result: Energy Fuels ranks among the lowest CapEx and OpEx costs for rare earth oxide production and one of the lowest all-in costs for magnet rare earths ("NdPr") in the world.

Their fixed costs on the mill totaled up 421M, under what was expected.

They are now able to process at $29.39/kg NdPr; the going price is near $86/kg, and the pentagon price floor is $110/kg, for comparison's sake.

Because of this, the mill should be able to be profitable in year one, and will take a few years to ramp up, hitting full steam ahead in nine years.

The report shows estimates of 33% IRR on the lifecycle of the project, with a steady decline in OpEx necessary to keep profits up. This, of course, assumes a fairly linear rise in rare earth prices, which I doubt -- I would assume that there is a lot of variability in these projections due to the potential for massive price shocks, like when Washington intervenes in the market.

Combine this with a strong uranium market, which is still facing severe deficits in supply due to the lack of expanding mines and the sanctions with Russia, which is a major supplier of uranium fuel to the west. Energy Fuels' estimates are for a fairly linear progression in uranium pricing, as supply deficits widen over time.

Here is the last five years, and then projections from TradeTech for the pricing of U3O8 uranium via Energy Fuels' presentation.

Here's a summary of the report for the technically inclined:

Risks

This build-out period has cost a lot of money, and Energy Fuels had been using debt a decade ago. This chart starts at their peak debt, and when rates start to fluctuate in 2018, they paid off significant amounts of their long term debt. Then when rates fell to zero in 2020, they took advantage and paid their debts down to almost nothing. Instead of issuing new debt at high rates now, although they have done some raising, they issued new shares. Share counts jumped last year as they financed significant amounts through equity.

Share dilution is a risk for investors because they could be right and the company is successful, but they could be diluted out of their position and left with little gains at the end.

There is also the fact that all that profit is in the future, and is projected. Currently, Energy Fuels is not a profitable endeavor, and that's a major risk to shareholders. They will have to continue to try to fund operations without taking on expensive debt or diluting shareholders out of their returns.

Last year was very expensive, as they were putting a lot of money into new projects and getting these plants online. Their quarterly deficits haven't been bigger in recent memory, but that is the cost of building out infrastructure. Nevertheless, it is an important risk for investors to consider; they are not going to get any dividends or passed-through earnings for a long time, and they are relying on earnings to justify an impossibly high price-to-earnings of 107x.

That makes this stock very speculative (that's why I'm invested in it!), which warrants the caution I like to give whenever I cover stocks like this, paraphrasing a mentor of mine.

Position sizing is often more important than security selection.

Based on what we've seen in the latest report from UUUU, it looks like they will be able to bring their earnings up quickly, and bring the P/E ratio down fast. When that happens, the price may jump, and investors willing to take the risk and speculate on that future, may be rewarded.

I am invested in UUUU, but my position is sized so that a long drawdown (which happens with stocks like these) won't harm my overall financial health. This is a stock that is most suitable for aggressive investors willing to make a bet on the macro and micro pictures aligning to favor Energy Fuels. I believe they do, but investors who cannot bear the risk of a 30%+ drawdown could be shaken out of the position too early. That psychology risk is important to consider as well.

That 30% figure isn't arbitrary. Between my last update in October and this one, Energy Fuels is up nearly 10%, but it fell over 30% from the date of the first article's publication between then and now.

Conclusion

The prospects for Energy Fuels are bright in my view, with bipartisan support in Washington through new legislation and executive actions in both rare earths and uranium (via nuclear power). Compound this with a recent report showcasing how efficient Energy Fuels has become as an individual firm, make this stock a Strong Buy to me still even at its elevated P/E ratio.

There are great risks, and position sizing is an important factor in an investment made in this stock. However, I believe that the next few years hold significant gains for Energy Fuels and the market is still underpricing its potential for growth. Washington has created a distorted market, and one they cannot back out of easily. Competition with the world's other great superpower is fierce, and seemingly supported by both Democrats and Republicans. Regime change in the midterms will not slow support for this sector.

Energy Fuels is on the path to consistent profitability, a fantastic sign; it will still take some quarters to get there, but the foundation has been laid. I am very bright about the stock's future.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: RSPN: Industrials Dashboard For January
data.description:
This article offers a top-down analysis of the industrial sector based on value, quality, and momentum metrics. It may also help analyze sector ETFs like Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Industrials ETF (RSPN) and Industrial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLI), whose largest holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italics describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each subsector: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross MaRSPNn ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non-available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set into a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively as EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for transportation in the table below is the 11-year average of the median earnings yield in transportation companies. The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.

Current data

The next table shows the metrics and scores as of last week's closing. Columns stand for all the data defined above.

Value and Quality chart

The next chart plots the Value and Quality Scores by subsector (higher is better).

Evolution since last month

The most notable change is a deterioration in value score for the aerospace & defense subsector.

Momentum

The next chart plots momentum based on median returns by subsector.

Interpretation

Based on my latest S&P 500 dashboard, industrials as a whole are one of the most overvalued sectors, along with materials and technology. However, the transportation industry is close to its 11-year averages in valuation and quality. Other subsectors are overvalued by 26% to 40%, aerospace & defense being the most overpriced. Overvaluation may be partly offset by a good quality score for building/equipment.

Focus on RSPN

Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Industrials ETF (RSPN) was launched on 11/01/2006 and tracks the S&P 500 Equal Weight Industrials Index. RSPN has a portfolio of 80 stocks and an expense ratio of 0.40%, while its capital-weighted counterpart XLI charges only 0.08% with the same holdings.

Positions are reset in equal weight at every quarterly rebalance. They are now between 1.06% and 1.52%, with 14% in the top 10 holdings, listed below. These are the constituents with the highest price return since the most recent rebalance. Company-specific risk is significantly lower than for XLI, whose top 10 positions represent 39% of assets, the heaviest one weighting 6.7%.

In summary, RSPN offers lower company risk, slightly better value and similar long-term returns and volatility compared to capital-weighted funds in the sector. It is well-suited for long-term investors seeking a balanced portfolio in industrials, but traders will prefer XLI’s higher trading volumes.

Dashboard List

I use the first table to calculate value and quality scores. It may also be used in a stock-picking process to check how companies stand among their peers. For example, the EY column tells us that a transportation company with an earnings yield above 0.0456 (or price/earnings below 21.93) is in the better half of the subsector regarding this metric. A Dashboard List is sent every month to Quantitative Risk & Value subscribers, with the most profitable companies standing in the better half among their peers regarding the three valuation metrics at the same time. The stocks below are part of the list sent to subscribers a few weeks ago.

data.title: Uber: Buy The Dip As Delivery Services Accelerate
data.description:
In general, I recommend caution in investing in the stock market in 2026. Valuation multiples look incredibly stretched, especially among large-cap tech stocks. That said, if we deploy careful stock selection, there are still great deals to be had, specifically among potential rebound plays.

Uber Technologies, Inc. (UBER), to me, is looking especially promising after a recent ~10% dip from near-term highs just shy of $100. Investors remain nervous on the company’s AV rollout, while also expressing caution around a slower macroeconomy.

In reality, Uber’s results don’t show any meaningful slowdown at all despite macro headwinds. I last wrote a "Buy" opinion on Uber in October, when the stock was trading closer to $94. Today, I’m happy to add to my position at lower prices, especially given the recent acceleration in delivery results. I reiterate my "Buy" rating here.

A couple of catalysts are worth spotlighting to emphasize the bull case for Uber. In particular, I’ve long since moved past thinking of Uber as merely a ride-hailing app. It’s true market opportunity is the overall convenience economy - whisking people and objects around utilizing its massive transportation network.

The biggest illustration of this is in grocery delivery. Recently, Uber has achieved meaningful acceleration in its deliveries segment, which is largely driven by the company’s heightened focus on grocery. Uber began with takeout orders, but the market for delivery is actually substantially larger. As shown below, the company believes the annual grocery delivery market opportunity is $10 trillion. That’s 5x the size of the $2 trillion restaurant delivery market. And though Uber is already the leading global delivery company, its quarterly delivery gross bookings of ~$23 billion implies it's still only ~1% penetrated into this opportunity.

We should also mention the company’s freight business. Now, of course, there are severe macro headwinds here at hand. Not only is the slowing consumer economy driving weakness in logistics, but tariffs are also reducing cross-border shipments into the U.S. Still, when the economy stabilizes, Uber has already built up an expansive freight business that is just on the cusp of breakeven profitability.

Despite its ~20% top-line growth and its upcoming catalysts in rolling out AVs (which should, at scale, reduce cost per mile driven for every booking), Uber still trades at a reasonable valuation. As shown below, after its recent crash, the stock is valued at ~16x adjusted EBITDA. To me, that’s more than a reasonable price to pay for the company’s current pace of >30% adjusted EBITDA growth.

Uber does indeed trade at a slightly richer multiple than Lyft (LYFT). Lyft is presenting a greater risk on the mobility side, as its rideshare bookings growth has closed the gap to Uber’s. Still, Uber’s overall market opportunity is far larger than Lyft's due to its wider portfolio of delivery services plus an enlarged global presence. Uber still sits at a much cheaper multiple than DoorDash (DASH), which I find reassuring.

Use the recent dip in Uber as a buying opportunity.

Q3 Download

Let’s now go through Uber’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The bookings trend in the company’s mobility segment are shown below.

Segment gross bookings grew 19% on a constant currency basis, up to $25.11 billion. We note here there is basically no evidence of any macro-driven slowdown: gross bookings accelerated a point versus 18% growth in Q2.

We think it’s prudent to note here that Uber’s competition in the U.S. is heating up. Lyft’s gross bookings growth of 19% in Q3 finally caught up to Uber’s growth rate. We note as well that Lyft’s recent acquisition of Freenow also expands its competitive presence into key European markets. That said, we don’t necessarily see evidence that the market size is limited here; clearly rideshare is still large enough for Uber to still accelerate its bookings profile. Also of note is the fact that Lyft is also selectively targeting certain niche markets (such as college campuses away from urban metro areas) where competition isn’t as dense. There’s enough room here for a “Coke and Pepsi” situation of two major rideshare players in the U.S.

In the delivery segment, Uber shone even brighter. As shown below, delivery gross bookings accelerated to 24% growth, up four points sequentially from 4% growth in Q2.

Again, here, a competitive assessment is apt. Main rival DoorDash saw two points of sequential acceleration to 25% growth (from 23% in Q2), slightly faster than Uber (but also accelerating slightly less). We note DoorDash and Lyft have a partnership between Dash Pass and Lyft Pink, giving both rideshare and delivery benefits to compete against Uber One’s all-in-one appeal.

Also of note: Amazon (AMZN) is expanding the same-day delivery coverage of its Amazon Fresh network. Amazon Fresh and Whole Foods both remain massive grocery players that are off-limits to rideshare companies, remaining within Amazon Prime’s walled garden.

Overall profitability for Uber rose 33% y/y to $2.26 billion in adjusted EBITDA, representing a 4.5% margin against bookings that improved 40bps y/y.

The company’s improving margin is a strong signal that despite competition in all segments, the company isn’t leaning in too heavily on incentives and promotions to maintain market share.

Q4 Look-Ahead And Key Takeaways

Uber is next set to report Q4 earnings at some point in early February. To me, these are the core items to watch:

  • How does Uber’s bookings growth compare to peers? Mobility and delivery both grew in line with key peers in Q3. Moving faster than peers would be a very strong indicator that will provide upside for the stock.
  • Margin trends. Amid a tighter macro, competitors may be offering more flash deals and promotions, forcing Uber to compete.
  • AV development. We’re also on the lookout for any anecdotal remarks on AV rollout.

All in all, I think Uber's recent trends and its trajectory more than justify a ~16x adjusted EBITDA multiple that can re-rate higher. Stay long here and "Buy" the dip.

data.title: China's 2025 Rebound: Why Selectivity Has Mattered
data.description:
By Hyun Kang

After years of uncertainty and underperformance, Chinese equities delivered a strong rebound in 2025. Improvements in investor sentiment - supported in part by advances in AI and semiconductor technology - helped rebuild confidence, while the MSCI China Index reached decade-high levels late in the year.

MSCI China Index and MSCI ACWI Index Year-to-Date Total Local Returns

Source: WisdomTree, MSCI, FactSet. Returns from 12/31/2024-12/31/2025. Past performance is not indicative of future results. You cannot invest directly in an index.

Policymakers implemented a series of targeted monetary, fiscal, and capital-market measures aimed at stabilizing growth and restoring confidence. Unlike past cycles, these actions were incremental and focused on risk containment rather than broad-based reflation.

Even so, China's equity gains in 2025 were not merely a policy-driven rally. Valuations began the year at deeply discounted levels, and as investor confidence improved, equities were repriced higher, narrowing the gap versus global markets.

Beyond policy support, investor sentiment has also been lifted by the resilience of China's technology sector and a faster-than-expected easing of U.S.-China tariff frictions. Plans for multiple high-level meetings between the two countries' leaders in 2026—potentially as many as four—have added to a sense of improving dialogue. A modest appreciation of the renminbi may also provide a tailwind for Chinese equities.

Even after this repricing, Chinese equities continue to trade at meaningful discounts relative to global equities, as measured by the MSCI ACWI Index. While valuation remains supportive, the renewed interest in China has been driven less by absolute cheapness and more by confidence in the resilience of its technology sector and supply-chain positioning.

Historical Price to Earnings

Source: WisdomTree, MSCI, FactSet. Data from 11/30/2015-12/31/2025. Past performance is not indicative of future results. You cannot invest directly in an index.

At the company level, Chinese equities continue to trade at marked valuation discounts, most notably relative to U.S. equities. The largest constituents of the MSCI China Index trade at substantially lower forward multiples than those of the S&P 500, despite stronger forward earnings growth expectations.

Forward Valuation and Earnings Growth: S&P 500 vs. MSCI China Top Constituents

Source: WisdomTree, S&P, MSCI, FactSet. Data as of 12/31/2025. Past performance is not indicative of future results. You cannot invest directly in an index.

Importantly, China's rebound has not been uniform across ownership structures. Government ownership can meaningfully influence corporate priorities, often creating trade-offs between policy objectives and shareholder outcomes.

WisdomTree's ex-State-Owned Enterprises approach focuses on companies with limited government ownership, where firms tend to operate with greater autonomy and stronger alignment with market incentives. By excluding state-owned enterprises (SOEs), this framework seeks to reduce governance and capital-allocation risks.

The WisdomTree China ex-State-Owned Enterprises Index, tracked by the WisdomTree China ex-State-Owned Enterprises Fund (CXSE), has had a very strong year in 2025, outperforming its benchmark, the MSCI China Index, by 574 basis points year-to-date. Most of this outperformance can be attributed to its overweight to non-SOEs (ex-SOEs) and underweight to SOEs, with 328 basis points of attribution driven by allocation effects.

Year-to-Date Index Performance Attribution

Source: WisdomTree, MSCI, FactSet. Returns from 12/31/2024-12/31/2025. Past performance is not indicative of future results. You cannot invest directly in an index.

CXSE's outperformance has not been limited to China benchmarks alone. In 2025, the strategy also outperformed developed and emerging market peers, underscoring that its results were not simply a byproduct of China's rebound, but of how exposure was constructed.

Year-to-Date Cumulative Performance

Source: WisdomTree, S&P, MSCI, FactSet. Returns from 12/31/2024-12/31/2025. You cannot invest directly in an index. Past performance is not indicative of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted.

China's experience in 2025 underscores that how investors gain exposure matters as much as whether they do. As valuations reset and confidence improved, performance dispersion reflected ownership structure and governance differences. CXSE's construction—tilting away from state ownership and toward market-oriented companies—has been well aligned with those dynamics.

Conclusion

Looking ahead, the U.S.–China relationship does not appear to be deteriorating, and China's technology sector has remained resilient. China's equity market is likely to continue to be defined by uneven macroeconomic recovery and meaningful dispersion. Investing in Chinese equities therefore requires a long-term view on China's technology sector, which is largely non-state owned. In that environment, strategies that emphasize governance, capital discipline, and market incentives may be better positioned to navigate ongoing uncertainty. CXSE offers one such approach for investors seeking a more selective way to access China's equity opportunity.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including possible loss of principal. Foreign investing involves special risks, such as risk of loss from currency fluctuation or political or economic uncertainty. The Fund focuses its investments in China, including A-shares, which include the risk of the Stock Connect program, thereby increasing the impact of events and developments associated with the region which can adversely affect performance. Investments in emerging or offshore markets are generally less liquid and less efficient than investments in developed markets and are subject to additional risks, such as risks of adverse governmental regulation and intervention or political developments. The Fund’s exposure to certain sectors may increase its vulnerability to any single economic or regulatory development related to such sector. As this Fund can have a high concentration in some issuers, the Fund can be adversely impacted by changes affecting those issuers. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Hyun Kang, Research Analyst

Hyun Kang joined WisdomTree in July 2022 as a Research Analyst. As a part of the Index team, he assists with the creation and maintenance of the firm’s indexes and supports the group’s research initiatives across various strategies. Hyun graduated from Carnegie Mellon University, with a B.S. in Business Administration and an additional major in Statistics and Machine Learning.

Original Post
